Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it rarely worked out that way. I'm someone who learns best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often end up building models or working through problems physically rather than sitting at a desk. Most of my actual studying happened in bursts, usually late at night when I finally forced myself to focus.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd go days without touching course material, then suddenly realize something was coming up and cram everything in. Regular schedules just don't stick for me—I find them boring and hard to maintain. The anxiety about deadlines would eventually kick in and that's when I'd actually get things done.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an assignment or wanting to check if grades were posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there was anything new, scroll around for a bit, and then close it because nothing grabbed my attention. The LMS felt like a chore most of the time, so I needed a specific reason to actually open it.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I never got into a rhythm where checking the LMS felt natural. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do. The interface just didn't engage me—I'm more of a hands-on person, and clicking through modules felt tedious and disconnected from actual learning.
Q5: The video content was probably the most useful because I could watch demonstrations and see things in action. I watched quite a bit of video material because it felt more engaging than reading. When instructors showed practical applications or worked through problems step by step, that actually helped me understand concepts better than just reading text.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums most of the time, even though I posted occasionally. Reading through everyone's comments felt like a waste of time when I could be doing something more productive. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because they seemed dry and I couldn't see how they connected to what I actually needed to know for exams.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate practical value or if it directly connected to assessment. If something was going to help me pass the exam or complete an assignment, I'd engage with it. But if it felt like busywork or abstract theory with no clear application, I'd zone out and move on to something else.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If it wasn't required, my brain just categorized it as unnecessary. Required activities at least had some external pressure attached to them, which I apparently need to get anything done. The optional stuff always got pushed aside because there were always more pressing things demanding my attention.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I downloaded lecture slides and PDFs early on and studied from those on my tablet without being connected. I also used YouTube videos and external tutorials that explained concepts in different ways. Sometimes I'd work through problems using physical materials—like actually building things or drawing diagrams on paper—which obviously doesn't show up anywhere online.
Q10: A lot of my studying happened with printed notes spread across my desk while I worked through problems by hand. I'd also study with a friend from the peer support group, and we'd quiz each other or explain concepts out loud. None of that shows up in any system, but those conversations probably helped me more than clicking through slides.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a video, then never go back to the LMS for that material again. It was just easier to have everything locally so I could study without needing internet or dealing with the platform. My offline study time was way higher than what the LMS would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I just couldn't bring myself to care. My anxiety was through the roof sometimes, and instead of motivating me, it just paralyzed me. I'd feel overwhelmed by everything I needed to do, get bored with the material, and end up doing nothing productive at all. Those were dark weeks where I barely engaged with anything.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or exhausted, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. The cognitive load of navigating the platform on top of actually learning felt like too much. I'd sometimes open it, stare at the screen, and close it without doing anything because I just didn't have the mental energy.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing in the background while scrolling through my phone, technically watching but not absorbing anything. Or I'd click through slides just to mark them as complete without really reading. It felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The boredom and anxiety created this weird combination where I'd avoid things because they stressed me out, but avoiding them made me more stressed. It's a terrible cycle. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd ignore the LMS for weeks and then frantically log in the night before something was due. My activity probably spiked dramatically around due dates and flatlined in between. Without that external pressure, I just couldn't motivate myself.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always positively. Good feedback made me feel like maybe I could do this, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to disengage entirely. I'd avoid checking grades sometimes because I was anxious about what I'd see. When feedback was constructive and specific, it helped me focus my efforts better.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid and didn't really accommodate different learning styles. Everything was laid out in a linear way that assumed everyone learns the same. I would have engaged more if there were more hands-on components or practical applications. The structure made it easy to fall behind and hard to catch up.
Q19: The heavy reliance on reading and clicking through modules made engagement harder for me as a kinesthetic learner. What helped was when there were interactive elements or when I could actually do something rather than just consume information. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't really create meaningful interaction.
Q20: I think some students might be genuinely trying to engage but struggling to produce output. They could be reading everything, watching all the videos, but then freezing when it comes to assignments because of anxiety or perfectionism. Or maybe they're exploring the content but not finding it translates into work they can submit. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That actually sounds like me. My exam score was decent even though my LMS activity was pretty low. I studied a lot offline, used external resources, and got help from the peer support program. The system only sees what happens on the platform, but learning happens in lots of places. You can't measure understanding just by counting clicks.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logs in and what they click on, but they can't show if that person is actually thinking or just going through motions. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers, the moments of actual understanding. They also can't capture emotional states—whether someone is engaged and curious or anxious and just trying to survive.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just pass. It's when you're curious and thinking about the material even when you're not studying. For me, engagement happens when I can connect concepts to real applications and do something with my hands. It's not about time spent—it's about mental investment.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much the peer support program actually helped me get through this course. Those conversations with other students kept me from completely giving up during my worst anxiety periods. It also wouldn't show all the physical, hands-on studying I did away from screens, or the fact that I was genuinely interested in some topics even when I was too bored or anxious to engage with the platform.
